application_techniques.name: Drip
application_techniques.tool_required: Stick, hardened brush, turkey baster, or punctured can
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Thinned to honey-like viscosity (approximately 500–5000 cP); flows but maintains cohesion
application_techniques.motion_type: Slow arcing or linear movement of applicator above horizontal canvas; full body movement
application_techniques.marks_produced: Sinuous lines of variable width, loops, coils, beads at endpoints; 1–15mm width typical
application_techniques.learning_curve: Moderate — feel for viscosity and movement speed takes practice

application_techniques.name: Pour
application_techniques.tool_required: Container with pour spout, bucket, coffee can, pitcher; no brush needed
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Very thin, near-watercolor (50–500 cP); must flow freely for staining effect
application_techniques.motion_type: Canvas tilted and rotated to direct flow; minimal artist movement — gravity does the work
application_techniques.marks_produced: Large pools, rivers, stained halos; soft bleeding edges on raw canvas; no impasto
application_techniques.control_level: Low — direction controlled by tilt, exact form not
application_techniques.learning_curve: Low to start, high mastery — reading canvas flow and timing is expert skill

application_techniques.name: Brush Application
application_techniques.tool_required: Bristle or soft brush, range from 1/2-inch detail to 8-inch house-painting brush
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Variable — buttery to thinned depending on mark type; full range
application_techniques.motion_type: Directional strokes, scumbling, stippling, washing, loaded dragging; most versatile technique
application_techniques.marks_produced: Gestural strokes, washes, scumbled texture, controlled lines, impasto if loaded; range from calligraphic to broad sweep
application_techniques.energy_level: Variable — low for washes, very high for sweeping gestural work
application_techniques.control_level: Variable — wash technique highly controlled; gestural sweeps low control
application_techniques.learning_curve: High — brush technique encompasses the full range of traditional and experimental application

application_techniques.name: Palette Knife / Painting Knife
application_techniques.tool_required: Steel palette knife with flexible or rigid blade; various shapes (trowel, lozenge, fan); putty knife for large-scale
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Tube consistency or slightly softened with medium; stiff enough to hold knife marks
application_techniques.motion_type: Spread, scrape, press, twist; knife held at varying angles; can be used to mix on canvas
application_techniques.marks_produced: Flat or textured impasto planes; sharp edges where knife terminates; ridged texture; scrape marks revealing lower layers
application_techniques.control_level: Medium — direction controlled, edge quality variable
application_techniques.learning_curve: Moderate — knife behavior with thick paint is distinct from brush; requires calibration

application_techniques.name: Spray / Aerosol
application_techniques.tool_required: Commercial spray gun (air-powered), aerosol can, or atomizer; appropriate ventilation required
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Very thin, spray-gun viscosity (50–200 cP); water-based or solvent-based depending on equipment
application_techniques.motion_type: Controlled sweeping of spray pattern across surface; distance from canvas changes dispersion pattern
application_techniques.marks_produced: Soft atomized gradients; mist overlay on existing layers; subtle tone shifts; no hard edges possible
application_techniques.control_level: Medium — spray pattern consistent but atomized edge is inherently soft
application_techniques.learning_curve: Moderate — spray equipment setup and pressure calibration requires practice

application_techniques.name: Scrape
application_techniques.tool_required: Palette knife, putty knife, rigid squeegee, straight-edge metal ruler
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Wet paint on canvas; works best with paint freshly applied; thicker paint can be scraped dry
application_techniques.motion_type: Blade drawn firmly across wet paint surface at low angle; single pass recommended for clean result
application_techniques.marks_produced: Paint removed or redistributed revealing lower layers; striations along scrape direction; ridge of accumulated paint at blade terminus
application_techniques.control_level: Medium — blade direction controlled, resulting texture emergent
application_techniques.learning_curve: Moderate — understanding what will be revealed requires knowledge of the painting's history

application_techniques.name: Sponge Application
application_techniques.tool_required: Natural sea sponge, synthetic foam sponge; different textures produce different marks
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Medium — fluid enough to load into sponge but not so thin as to drip uncontrollably
application_techniques.motion_type: Pressing, dabbing, rolling; directional wiping for scumble effects; loaded pressure varies mark character
application_techniques.marks_produced: Irregular textured marks; soft stippled patterns; atmospheric smudging; absorbent surface creates distinctive cellular texture

application_techniques.name: Rag / Cloth Application
application_techniques.tool_required: Torn canvas rag, cheesecloth, linen, cotton muslin; varying textures available
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Variable — thin for wipe-on glazing; medium for textured application
application_techniques.motion_type: Wiping, rubbing, dragging; rag bunched for stipple; flat rag for wide coverage
application_techniques.marks_produced: Wiped glaze effects; rough dragged texture; soft atmospheric color; random fabric texture pattern transferred to surface
application_techniques.control_level: Medium-High — rag allows broad even coverage

application_techniques.name: Hand / Finger Application
application_techniques.tool_required: Bare hands; fingers, palm, or full hand; requires appropriate paint safety (avoid lead-based pigments)
application_techniques.paint_consistency_required: Variable — fingers can manage from thin glaze to thick impasto
application_techniques.motion_type: Pressing, spreading, pulling, smearing; the hand's anatomy determines mark shape — finger for line, palm for area, knuckle for texture
application_techniques.marks_produced: Fingerprint texture unique to the artist; organic flowing forms following hand anatomy; direct imprint marks; warm, intimate touch quality
application_techniques.control_level: Medium — the hand's anatomy is the constraint and character
application_techniques.learning_curve: None — the most primal mark-making method

canvas_prep_methods.name: Raw Canvas (Unprimed)
canvas_prep_methods.description: Canvas used with no preparation — directly off the roll; tightly woven linen or cotton duck
canvas_prep_methods.process: Canvas stretched and stapled to stretcher bars; no sizing or primer applied; paint applied directly to fiber
paint_compatibility: highly thinned oil in mineral spirits
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: Very high — paint wicks into fiber immediately
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: Stained, absorbed color that is part of the canvas fabric; characteristic of Frankenthaler's Mountains and Sea; edges soft and bleed slightly
advantages: eliminates figure/ground by unifying color and support
disadvantages: oil paint on raw canvas acidifies fibers over time (archival concern)
canvas_prep_methods.archival_quality: Poor-Moderate for oil (acid damage to fiber over decades); Good for acrylic

canvas_prep_methods.name: Rabbit Skin Glue Sizing
canvas_prep_methods.description: Traditional sizing that seals canvas fibers without fully priming; creates semi-absorbent surface
canvas_prep_methods.process: Glue dissolved in warm water (50g/L); applied warm to stretched canvas with wide brush; one to two coats; sand lightly between coats when dry
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: Moderate — sized canvas retains some absorbency but prevents full fiber penetration
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: Semi-absorbent; paint sits just at surface and in upper fiber layer; characteristic of Rothko's grounds
advantages: allows wash layering without full absorption
beautiful surface for translucent oil washes
disadvantages: hygroscopic — swells and contracts with humidity causing canvas tension issues
rabbit skin is reversible with moisture

canvas_prep_methods.name: Oil Ground
canvas_prep_methods.description: Traditional white or tinted ground based on lead white or titanium white in linseed oil
materials: lead white or titanium white in linseed oil
canvas_prep_methods.process: Canvas sized first; oil ground applied in thin coat over dried size; allowed to dry minimum 2 weeks before painting
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: Low — paint sits on surface; relatively non-absorbent
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: Slightly textured, luminous white; paint slides easily; traditional 'painter's ground' of European tradition
advantages: luminous ground shows through transparent glazes
canvas_prep_methods.archival_quality: Excellent for oil over correctly applied lead or titanium ground

canvas_prep_methods.name: Acrylic Gesso
canvas_prep_methods.description: Synthetic polymer ground — calcium carbonate in acrylic binder; became standard from 1960s onward
canvas_prep_methods.process: Two to three coats applied to stretched or unstretched canvas; sand between dry coats; water-thin first coat for best adhesion
paint_compatibility: oil paint over fully cured acrylic (acceptable)
not suitable for very lean oil in first layers
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: Moderate — adjustable by number of coats; more coats = less absorption
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: White, evenly textured, consistent; universal in post-1960s studio practice
disadvantages: colors can appear slightly chalky if not adjusted
canvas_prep_methods.archival_quality: Very Good — well-tested archival record from 1950s onwards

canvas_prep_methods.name: Staining with Turpentine Wash (Toned Ground)
canvas_prep_methods.description: Canvas toned with a thin wash of oil paint in mineral spirits before working; sets a chromatic ground without full priming
materials: wide brush or rag for application
canvas_prep_methods.process: Oil paint thinned heavily in mineral spirits to watery consistency; applied broadly across raw or sized canvas; allowed to sink in and dry partially before working; can be applied to raw canvas for direct staining
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: High on raw canvas; moderate on sized canvas
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: Toned canvas with chromatic warmth or coolness established; eliminates the problem of working against pure white
advantages: eliminates pure white ground which can distort color judgment
disadvantages: not archival over raw canvas (oil on raw canvas)
must be fully dry before further work
canvas_prep_methods.archival_quality: Poor over raw canvas (long-term); Good over properly sized canvas

canvas_prep_methods.name: Masonite / Fiberboard Ground
canvas_prep_methods.description: Rigid hardboard (Masonite) used as painting support instead of canvas; can be primed or painted directly
materials: acrylic gesso or rabbit skin glue size
canvas_prep_methods.process: Panel cut to size; smooth side or textured side used; if priming: 2-3 coats of gesso sanded between; rigid support requires no stretcher
canvas_prep_methods.absorption_rate: Low to none on tempered Masonite; moderate on untempered after sizing
canvas_prep_methods.resulting_surface: Very smooth (smooth side) or slightly textured (rough side); excellent for enamel and drip techniques
advantages: rigid — no canvas bounce or flex
excellent for enamel pour and drip techniques
flat surface ideal for Pollock's floor technique
disadvantages: heavy and rigid — awkward to transport and store at large scale
canvas_prep_methods.archival_quality: Good to Very Good; rigid support prevents cracking in thick paint

paint_types.name: Oil Paint
paint_types.binder: Linseed oil, safflower oil, or walnut oil
paint_types.drying_time: Slow — surface dry in 1-7 days; fully cured in 6 months to years depending on pigment and thickness
paint_types.dilution_medium: Mineral spirits or turpentine (for thinning); stand oil or damar varnish (for medium)
paint_types.viscosity: Buttery to stiff; varies by pigment; can be thinned to watercolor consistency with mineral spirits
paint_types.layering_behavior: Fat over lean rule essential — early thin layers must be leaner than later fat layers to prevent cracking; glazing possible when layers fully dry
paint_types.archival_quality: Excellent when correctly applied; the art-historical standard for longevity
paint_types.historic_use: Dominant medium in Western painting from 15th century; used by all AbEx artists in 1940s-50s; de Kooning, Kline, Motherwell, Rothko primarily oil painters
disadvantages: slow drying creates logistical challenges at large scale

paint_types.name: Acrylic Paint
paint_types.drying_time: Fast — surface dry in 20-60 minutes; usable for overpainting in hours
paint_types.dilution_medium: Water (for thinning); acrylic medium, gel, or retarder (for extending); matte or gloss medium affects surface
paint_types.viscosity: Variable by formulation; fluid acrylics comparable to thinned oil; heavy body acrylics comparable to oil tube paint
paint_types.layering_behavior: Excellent — layers dry quickly allowing rapid building; no fat-over-lean restriction; acrylic on acrylic bonds well; oil cannot go over acrylic without medium
paint_types.archival_quality: Very Good — flexible when dry, resistant to cracking; chemical stability excellent; archival studies ongoing as medium is newer
paint_types.historic_use: Developed commercially in 1950s; Frankenthaler and Louis began using Magna (acrylic) from 1953; standard medium by 1960s; Warhol, Noland, post-1960 AbEx artists
disadvantages: color shift on drying (slightly darker when wet)
lower solubility for oil paint on top without preparation

paint_types.name: Enamel Paint (Commercial)
paint_types.binder: Alkyd resin or nitrocellulose in mineral spirits
paint_types.drying_time: Medium — surface dry in 2-8 hours; hard dry in 24-48 hours
paint_types.viscosity: Factory-made to specific consistency; house-paint enamel has drip-optimized viscosity; Pollock preferred Duco (Du Pont) and Krylon brands
paint_types.layering_behavior: Good — dries to hard, non-tacky surface; fast enough for same-day layering; alkyd over alkyd excellent
paint_types.archival_quality: Moderate — commercial formulations not designed for archival use; yellowing possible; some pigments less stable than artist-grade
paint_types.historic_use: Critically important for Pollock — the drip technique relied on enamel's specific drip-optimized viscosity; Kline used commercial black enamel; enamel's properties made the drip technique possible
advantages: available in industrial quantities and consistent viscosity
disadvantages: limited color range compared to artist pigments

paint_types.name: Watercolor
paint_types.drying_time: Fast — dry in minutes; re-wettable when dry
paint_types.viscosity: Very thin — always aqueous; flow determined by dilution
paint_types.layering_behavior: Excellent for glazing; transparent layers build chromatic depth; paint can be lifted when wet; dried layers can be rewet and moved
paint_types.archival_quality: Good to Excellent — high-quality watercolor pigments are very stable; paper support archival concerns greater than paint
paint_types.historic_use: Less central to AbEx but used in studies and works on paper; Twombly used watercolor extensively in works on paper
advantages: luminous color from white paper showing through
disadvantages: very low viscosity limits layering opacity
requires paper support (different from canvas-based AbEx)

paint_types.name: Gouache
paint_types.binder: Gum arabic with chalk or zinc oxide opacifier
paint_types.drying_time: Fast — dry in minutes; water-soluble when dry
paint_types.viscosity: Medium — thicker than watercolor; can be applied opaquely
paint_types.layering_behavior: Opaque layers possible; can be thinned for washes; upper layers can lift lower layers if both rewet; must be handled carefully
paint_types.archival_quality: Good — archival with quality pigments; but re-solubility when wet is a conservation concern
paint_types.historic_use: Motherwell used gouache extensively in collage works and preparatory studies; also used by many AbEx artists for works on paper
disadvantages: not suitable for oil-based layering systems

paint_types.name: Mixed Media
paint_types.layering_behavior: Requires understanding of compatibility; oil over acrylic possible; acrylic over dried oil possible; enamel over oil possible; testing recommended
paint_types.archival_quality: Variable — archival quality determined by weakest-archival component; some mixed combinations create long-term conservation problems
paint_types.historic_use: Pollock routinely mixed oil, enamel, aluminum paint, sand, and debris; de Kooning mixed oil, enamel, and commercial paints for specific viscosities; Twombly combined oil, crayon, pencil, and house paint
advantages: expands expressive range beyond any single medium
viscosity contrasts between media create unique layering effects
Pollock's drip technique required specific mixed viscosity profiles
disadvantages: attribution of specific media in existing works requires technical analysis
